segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]
JOLANDA VERHOEF: Hi, everyone.
[CHEERING]
[APPLAUSE]
Welcome.
Thanks for joining us today.
My name is Jolanda.

And I'll be joined later
by John and Jingyu.

And together, we'll share
what is new and upcoming
in Android development.

I'll start with a
bit of a deep dive

into what we're
doing to make you,
as developers, more productive.

And then John and Jingyu
will cover the wide variety

of changes and
improvements that we're

making to help you build
excellent apps across devices.

And because I get
to start, of course,

I'm going to start with my
all-time favorite topic, Jetpack
Compose.
[CHEERING]

Now, I was actually
preparing this talk,

and I was looking
for some inspiration.

And so I watched the 2019
talk, the What's New in Android
talk back then.

And it made me realize that
we've come such a long way.

Back then, we introduced
Compose as this new UI toolkit
for all of you to try out.

And now Compose is our
recommended toolkit
for building UI on Android.

And year over year, we
see the adoption grow.

We are now at 60% of the top
1,000 apps using it and loving
it in production.

And Compose gives us the
features, the performance,

the stability, and
the libraries that you

need to help build you
beautiful adaptive apps,

and also to build those
apps faster so that you

can be focusing on those things
that truly matter to your users.

With the stable
release of Compose,

we've added lots of
new functionalities,

with more to come in
the alphas as well.

And today, I want to highlight
a few important ones for you.
So let's start with autofill.

Autofill allows users of your
app to automatically fill out

form fields with
personal information

that they filled out
some time before.

And you've been asking for
autofill support in Compose
for quite some time now.

And we've actually been working
on it for quite some time
now, to be honest.

But it was quite
a difficult thing

to implement because
we needed to do

quite a big rewrite of our
internal semantic system.
We continued.
We did that rewrite.
It's done.

And so the basic
autofill APIs are

available for you
to use in the latest
stable version of Compose.
So let's take a look, right?

Here, you see a
typical login screen

with two text fields,
username and password.

And those two text
fields both play a role

in authentication,
which we somehow have
to convey to the autofill APIs.
So how would we implement that?

First, we get a
semantics modifier.

You've been using this probably
in some other cases in your app
as well.

And inside, we have a
new ContentType property.

When you set that
ContentType property,

you can give it the role
of the authentication
that that text field has.

So in this case, I'm
setting the first one

to the type Username and then
the second one to type Password.

And that's all we need
to make autofill work.

Now, if you use the
alpha version of Compose,

this is actually even
easier because we've

added a new modifier,
ContentType,

which lets you just set this
directly with one line of code.

Next up is autosizing text, or
making your text automatically

adapt to the size
of its container.

This is as easy as adding
this new AutoSize parameter
to your text composable.

And you can even configure it
with minimum and maximum font

sizes or with the
granularity of each step.

Another modifier we've
added is animate bounds,

which lets you
automatically animate

the position and the size of
a composable in its container.

This is similar to animated
content size, which
you might already be using.

But this one does both
the size and the position

at the same time, given that
you're in a lookahead scope.

Then another new modifier, this
time to do visibility tracking.

In the latest stable
version of Compose,
we added onLayoutRectChanged.

This is a low-level
API that you can

use to track a composable's
position within either

its roots, the
window, or the screen.

And it unlocks various
use cases, mostly related

to visibility tracking
or position tracking.

Now, it's similar to
onGloballyPositioned,

which you might already be
using to track the location.

But this one is more
performance out of the box.

And it allows you to add
throttle or debouncing, which

makes it OK to use for
use cases like tracking

the location of an item while
you're scrolling a lazy list.

And in the alpha
versions of Compose,

we've added some
higher-level APIs

on top of this low-level
API, which are very, very
specific for various use cases.

So first of all, we added
onVisibilityChanged,

which gives you a callback any
time the visibility of that

composable changes, so it
enters or it exits the viewport.

This is great for use cases like
automatically playing or pausing
a video when it comes into view.

And we're actually
using it in Androidify.

We've also added
onFirstVisible, which

is great for use cases
like impression tracking

because it gives a callback
any time a composable has

been in view for a
certain amount of time.

So you can already start using
this stable onLayoutRectChanged

modifier in your stable
version of Compose.

And then you can use
these higher-level APIs
when you use the alpha version.

You can learn more
about these APIs

and many more in
the two brand-new

technical talks that we're
releasing as part of this year's

I/O for best practices
in text input

and best practices
in accessibility.

But we haven't
been focusing only

on adding new
features to Compose.

And new features also shouldn't
be the only reason why you're
upgrading your Compose version.

Another consideration
is performance.

When you run your app on
older versions of Compose,

you might sometimes
see some jank.

This is what some of
you have mentioned.

When you run older
versions, and you run them

on lower-end devices,
there might be some jank.

In this case, we're using an
internal benchmark project.

And in that internal
benchmark project,
we've just built an app.

And we're running it with
versions of Compose from 2023.

We're running it on
a Pixel 3A device.

And we're seeing jank
rates of about 2%.

Now, what we did is we kept the
source code exactly the same,

but we updated the
version of Compose.

And so what we see is that
with later versions of Compose,

this jank rate already
drops to about 1%.

Then, if we update the
Compose version to March 2025,

this drops to less
than half a percent.

And with the latest
stable version of Compose,
we hardly have any jank left.
We're at less than 0.1%.
[APPLAUSE]

Now, this is just
one example benchmark

that demonstrates all the work
that our engineers have been

doing in the past years to
make the performance of Compose
better.

But our engineers are
constantly finding and applying
other optimizations as well.

For example, we're introducing
pausable composition,

which splits the composition
over several frames.

We are offloading some of the
harder work of the main thread,
starting with text measurements.

And we're allowing you to
customize the prefetching
of items in a lazy list.

So between these new features
that I just mentioned

and the performance
updates, now really

is a time for you to prioritize
upgrading your Compose version

and get access to all
of these added benefits.

Now, remember that I said that
60% of the top 1,000 apps are
using Compose.

Several of those are
actually Google apps.

Google Photos, Maps,
and Drive, and more,

have been using Compose
in production for years.

And recently, we've been
working with these app teams

to improve the stability
of our Compose releases

by catching bugs at an earlier
stage because some of you

have mentioned that upgrading
Compose versions can sometimes

be difficult. So let me
show you a little bit more
about how we did this.

What you're used to is that
whenever we have a Compose

change, we merge this into
the Compose main branch,

and then it goes through
this alpha, beta, RC,
and stable release cycle.

But for Compose--
for Google apps,

this actually works a
little bit differently

because all of the
Google apps share

a single version of
Compose among them.

And we used to update that
version about once a month.

But recently, we've
been working to update
that about once every day.

So any change in Compose that
makes it into the main branch

actually makes it to all of
these Google apps within days

and can make it to millions
of users within a week.

Now, that also means
that for new features,

we are adding feature
flags to make sure

that we allow for early testing
by these Google apps or by you
in alpha versions of Compose.
And you can test them.

And you can let us know if there
are any performance regressions
or bugs with the implementation.

And so we have this quick
iteration of testing out
new functionality.

And at some point, whenever
the changes make it

into a beta or a stable
version of Compose,

they've often been running in
production in these Google Apps

with millions of users
for weeks, if not months.

That should give you
a lot more certainty

in upgrading to the latest
Compose stable version whenever
it comes out.

Now, another stability
improvement that we did

is that we did a big sweep of
all of our experimental APIs.

You shared with us
that having too many

of these experimental
APIs can sometimes

make the API surface feel
a little bit less stable.
So we went through them.

And for many of the
APIs, we cleaned them up

and made sure that we
could stabilize them.

But some of them were actually
experimental for several years

now because we eventually wanted
to get around to fixing them.

But they're either good
enough as they are,

and we stabilize them now,
or we deprecated them,
and we will remove them for now.

So with that, we reduced
32% of the experimental APIs

over the course
of just one year.

And looking at some of the
biggest contributors, Foundation

and Compose UI, that number
even increases to 66%.

So this should
give you much more

security in using stable APIs
when you're using Compose.

And also, we plan to remove
even more of those in the future
as well.

Now, so far, I've
been talking about--
[APPLAUSE]
Thank you.

Now, so far, I've been
mentioning Compose.

And I've been talking about
all the core modules--

Foundation, Runtime,
UI, et cetera.

But actually, we are working
on a wider suite of libraries.

Compose is part of the
set of Jetpack libraries.

And we've been working on
adding Compose supports on more
of these libraries as well.

You've heard a little bit about
this in the Developer Keynote.

But I wanted to expand a
little bit more in this talk.

So first of all, any
app has navigation.

And we've heard that
the Jetpack navigation

solution can sometimes be
challenging in a Compose world.

Especially when you're
dealing with the backstack

or with hoisting
state, it doesn't seem
to quite fit that use case.

And so we sat down, and we
re-envisioned what we think
Compose should look like--

what we think navigation should
look like in a Compose world.

And thus, we're introducing
Navigation 3, a first alpha

of a new artifact that
makes it much easier to work

with navigation in a Compose
app because this is built
with Compose and using Compose.

And so let me show you
what that library offers.

The library makes it
easy to do transitions

between different
destinations using animations,

to implement predictive back,
or to create shared element
transitions.

It has a flexible, layered
architecture that works--

that is adaptive out
of the box and works

across form factors,
including XR.

And it even has a material
design integration.

But most importantly, it
is fully customizable.

So if you need more
control, we give you
all the control you need.

You can, if you want to,
control the full backstack

and implement navigation
exactly the way
that it is needed for your app.
[APPLAUSE]

Now, this library is
currently in alpha.
So we're still working on it.

But we really want to make sure
that this is the best navigation
library we could be building.
So please go and check it out.

There's full
documentation available.

And we would really like
to hear from you if this
works for your use cases.

Then, another thing we heard
from you loud and clear

is that you want Compose
support for camera and media
experiences.

And so we've been working
on adding this support.

We have Media3 and CameraX
as our recommended solutions

for these experiences
in your Android apps.

And here as well,
we re-envisioned

what this should look
like in a Compose world.

So we didn't just wrap
Preview View or Player View
and call it a day.

We're actually building building
blocks from the ground up

and making sure that you can
build whatever your app needs,
as much or as little.

That, unfortunately, does mean
that it will take a little bit

longer to get to full parity
with Player View and Preview
View.

But it also means that you have
full control over your camera
or media experience.

So let me demonstrate
what we mean with that
for the Media3 artifacts.
Here, you see Androidify.

And Androidify has this
use case where we wanted
to show a tutorial video.
It's quite a trivial video.
We know the URL.
We know the aspect ratio.

The only thing we want
to do is automatically

play it and then show a
Play/Pause button on top of it

so this video can be paused
for accessibility purposes.
Let's implement it.

We start with a VideoPlayer
composable in our sample app.

And what you see is
that we're passing

in a Player instance, which is
coming from a Media3 library.

Then what we do is we
simply forward this Player

instance to the
PlayerSurface composable
as part of the new artifact.

And that Surface
composable actually
takes care of the rendering.
Nothing we have to do.

Then, to add a
Play/Pause button,

instead of calling the
library directly and letting

it render it for us, we
actually implement our own.
And we pass the Player into it.

So how does my Play/Pause
button look like?

Well, what do we do with
the Player instance?

We create a
PlayPauseButtonState.
This is part of the artifact.

And it allows us to
interact with the Player.

And it allows us to show
data from the Player.
So we control the UI.

But the artifact controls
all the pesky logic

of whether we should be showing
a Play or a Pause button
and what to do when we click it.
And with that, it works.
That's all.
[APPLAUSE]

And with that, you saw
how Compose makes it easy

and how it has all the features,
performance, stability,

and libraries for
you to build apps.
But UI is not all there is.

So let's move on to
the business layer.

Kotlin Multiplatform
allows you to--
[CHEERING]

--allows you to share business
logic across platforms.

As you can tell, it is
a very popular library.

And of course, we see this
in the community as well.

Only last year, there
were 900 new KMP libraries
being published.

And within Google,
we also support KMP.

And we make sure that where
it's possible and necessary,

and where it's logical to share
business logic in our Jetpack

suite of libraries,
we add KMP support.

So last year, we
already stood here,

and we discussed Room,
DataStore, and Collections.

This year, we're adding Saved
State, View Model, Lifecycle,

and Paging to that
list of stable support.

Now, what do I mean when
I say stable support?
Because KMP is vast.

There are so many platforms that
our code could be running on.

And so, within the Jetpack
library suites, what we've done

is we've divided this into
three different categories

or tiers of platforms, where
each tier has different kind
of commitments from our side.

So in this case, tier
1 not only has Android,

but we've added iOS
and JVM to this list.

With those three, you
can build a mobile app

that shares business logic
across Android and iOS
and across a Kotlin backend.

It has the full
support that you're

used to for Jetpack libraries
for Android, now also

available for these
other platforms.

And then tier 2 and tier 3 have
varying lower levels of support.

So let's say that you have
an Android or an iOS app,

and you want to start
sharing your business logic.
Where are you going to start?

My two colleagues,
Ben and Dustin,

have also created
a technical talk

that you can watch that helps
guide you through this process.

We also published
two new code labs

that guide you through
the process step by step.

And within Android Studio, we've
added a new KMP Module template.

So given that you have
an Android app already

in Android Studio,
you can get started

through this module
template as well.

Now, I'm talking about
developer productivity.

I discussed Kotlin
Multiplatform.
I discussed Compose.

Of course, I have to talk
about Gemini in Android Studio.

And in the Developer
Keynote, you already

heard about Journeys and
about the Version Update Agent
with some great demos.

And instead of me going
into more detail here,

I would like to
invite you to watch
the What's New in Tools talk.

With that, we've seen how
Kotlin Multiplatform, Compose,

and Gemini in Android Studio
help you be more productive.

So we can move on
to excellent apps

because your users want
to see excellent apps that

are safe and performant, that
work across devices, that

are delightful, and that
give you helpful experiences
through UI.

But before we handle all of
these five different topics

separately, I want to give
you an update about Android 16

because Android 16 is the
cornerstone of all of these five
pillars.

It creates beautiful
experiences.

But it also makes
you do some changes

to your code, which I know
can be difficult to handle.

So we've actually changed the
release cycle of Android 16.

Last year, we were
here talking to you

about a beta version
of Android 15.

Now we're here talking to
you about the actual release
of Android 16, coming very soon.

So any changes that
we're discussing today

are going to be very relevant
very soon for all of you.

Also, at the end of the
year, also a new change,

we're adding another
minor SDK release.

This one does not have any
breaking behavior changes, only

new APIs that you can opt
into if you want and can help
you build these excellent apps.

Now, throughout the
rest of this talk,

whenever we're discussing
any Android 16 changes,

to make it easier for
you to follow along,

we've added this
traffic light system.

So whenever we're talking
about an awesome new feature

or a new API, we will add this
green light that gives you

an indication that
you're allowed to update,

but we're not forcing
you to do anything.

Then orange means
a required change

but only when you update
your target SDK version.

So you are in charge
on timing on this one.

And finally, Android 16
required changes, the red ones,

are those that will be effective
regardless of your target SDK
version.

So when you see this
red symbol, you probably

want to look out and
start testing your app.

And with that, I would
like to hand it off to John

to start talking about
how to build safe apps.
Thank you.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
[APPLAUSE]

JOHN ZOELLER: I don't
know why I did that.

I've never boxed
a day in my life.
Hi, everybody.
I'm John Zoeller.

I work on Wear OS
and Android Identity.

And I'm here to talk to
you about excellent apps.

So let's go ahead
and start with safe.

And the first topic
is authentication.

Now, Credential Manager
is the key topic here.
This is our unified auth API.

It is a simple and secure
solution for users.

And it is interoperable
across the universe.

It supports credentials,
Sign In with Google

across all form factors,
passwords, also across all form

factors, and then passkeys with
phones, tablets, wear, and XR.
But this is table stakes, right?
You already know all this.

And this is What's
New in Android.

So we've got some new stuff
for Credential Manager.

First is digital
credentials verification,

or-- yeah, digital
credentials verification.

This allows you to verify
IDs for age content

checks, academic degrees,
all kinds of stuff like that.

And the best part
is it's built right
into the GetCredential flow.

So you're already used
to that kind of logic.

All you have to do
is furnish that API

call with a
DigitalCredentials object.

And it takes care
of the rest for you.

Next up, we've got the
Restore Credentials API.

Now, when a user
gets a new device,

it can get a little bit
frustrating to reauthenticate
into all of those apps, right?

They have to remember
their passwords.

They're like, what
did I sign in with?

So we've got Restore
Credentials for you.

Now, this is great
because it allows

users to auto-authenticate
into their app

on orientation, when
they set up a new device.

So this is a great option for
users, really helps engagement.

And it's the same
story as before.

It's just the
GetCredentials call.

You set it up with a
RestoreCredentials option.
And that's that.
Check out these resources.

You can learn more about
both of those topics.
All right, Privacy Sandbox.
Now, we know you love 3P code.
It's great.
You don't have to own it.
You don't have to maintain it.
It's nice and simple.

But you also don't
love it, right?

Because you got to maintain
someone else's privacy contract

or worry about someone
else's privacy contract.
So what are you going to do?

How are you going
to handle this?

Well, you're going to do the
same thing my little brother did

when he hid all of my toys for
me when I was six years old.

You're going to bury
it in a sandbox, baby,
a Privacy Sandbox, all right?

Privacy Sandbox allows you to
isolate 3P code in a dedicated,

isolated runtime
environment, all right?

You can isolate any code,
in fact, not just 3P code.

So this can be sensitive
logic, gaming subsystems, ads,
anything of that nature.

Just make sure you
understand that data

sharing between the runtime
and your app is not implicit.

You have to handle
it explicitly.

So as long as you handle that,
you're going to be all right.

The new news for
Privacy Sandbox is

we've got an all-new
learning pathway.

So if you're out here trying
to learn, Should I use it?

How am I going to use it? this
is your go-to resource here.
Take a look.

Even if you're familiar
with it, hopefully you
learned a thing or two.
All right, privacy and security.

Now, your users
expect that you're not

going to make any
compromises to their security
or to their privacy.

So we've got some new
features here to help.

We've got a lot of new features
in privacy and security.
I picked my favorite two.
But wow, there are a ton.

So first up, Android
Advanced Protection mode.

Now, this is a suite
of security features

that users can toggle
on in their device.
I'm not going to list them all.

But your app might be affected,
depending on what kind of app
you have.
So take a look into this.

And if you're
affected, you will need

to integrate UX with those
features to account for that.
It's really simple.
The code looks like this.

You just opt into the
permissions check.
The API is dead simple.

It's just two callbacks
and a Boolean.

So have a look at the
documentation for this
and give it a shot.

Next up is theft protection
with Identity Check.

Now, this is for apps that
have sensitive user actions.

Think like a banking
app, where you

need to check the amount
of money in your account.

This-- I see people
excited about this already.

This is great because if you're
in an untrusted location,

Identity Check is
going to enforce

an extra layer of
biometric security

so that you have to
put in your fingerprint

if you're in a location
where your device is not
familiar with.
So it adds a lot of security.

And once again, the
code is really nice.

You just check to see if
Identity Mode is active,
Identity Check is active.

And if you do, you add the
extra biometric prompt API,
like you're probably used to.
Oh, wow, this is a huge water.

The resources for
that are right there.
Next up, Health Connect.
All right, everybody.
Sit up straight.
Take a deep breath.
Be mindful.

I'm going to take a drink

to health--
if I can get this thing undone.
There we go.
Cheers.
All right.

So Health Connect
is great because it

allows you to share users'
health data between apps.

You're able to find
insights about things

like sleep, movement patterns,
all kinds of different health
data.
And it is growing rapidly.

We've got 50% active user growth
over just the last six months.

It's incredible to

Adoption is flourishing.
We've got new apps.

So this is an exciting
time for Health Connect.

And we want to help you
connect to this growth.
Da-da, tss!
No?
OK.
That one fell flat.
That's OK.
All right.

First up, first new thing
for Health Connect--
medical records APIs.

We've got a couple
new ones there.

Now, this helps you
consolidate health data.

You can read and write things
like allergies, immunizations,

and other kinds of health data
to enable richer experiences.

This helps make your
health apps more informed

and provides users with vital
everyday medical context.

It's nice to see there are
a couple different APIs
in this suite here.

But I'm going to direct you
towards the MedicalDataSource
API.

Now, I moonlight
as an MD, actually.

I've been doing brain
surgery on the side.
It's pretty easy.

And if I add my brain surgery
clinic to this MedicalDataSource

object, I can use it within
my app for all kinds of logic.
You should give it a try, too.

You can provide your users
all kinds of new data.

We're adding two new
data types as well.

First, we've got
background reads.

This helps you provide timely
insights to your users.

And second, we've
got history reads.

This allows you to help
your users spot trends

over time, which, if you've used
health apps, is really helpful.

The code for both of

You just need to check the

of either read type and
then, for background reads,

schedule a periodic
work builder.

And for history reads, just
make sure you set a data origin.

And we'll take care
of the rest for you.

So here are the resources
for both of those.
Take a look.
Next up, performant.
All right.

Before I start
performant, I got to say,

if your app is as
performant as this AC,
you can just take a nap.
You won.
All right.
Let's get started on performant.

So runtime performance-- this
is critical for a nice app.

I think all of
you know, when you

have great runtime performance,
it helps your Play Store rating.

You can earn really
good Play Store ratings

if your app is nice and
smooth and performant.

So the first feature
for this is R8.
